You are extracting a knowledge graph from a document chunk.

Document ID:
doc_000001

Chunk ID:
chunk_000001

Knowledge graph schema:
schema_version: kg_schema_v0.1

entity_types:
  - name: Concept
    required_fields: [id, canonical_name]
  - name: Document
    required_fields: [id, canonical_name]
  - name: Person
    required_fields: [id, canonical_name]

relation_types:
  - name: supports
    domain: Concept
    range: Concept
    head_arity: {min: 1, max: null}
    tail_arity: {min: 1, max: null}

  - name: defines
    domain: Document
    range: Concept
    head_arity: {min: 1, max: 1}
    tail_arity: {min: 1, max: null}

  - name: authored
    domain: Person
    range: Document
    head_arity: {min: 1, max: null}
    tail_arity: {min: 1, max: null}

Allowed entity types:
Concept, Document, Person

Allowed relation types:
supports, defines, authored

Text chunk:
# Knowledge Graphs

A knowledge graph is a structured representation of entities and their relationships.
Knowledge graphs provide a way to encode factual knowledge in a machine-readable format.
They are widely used in search engines, question answering systems, and recommendation engines.

Knowledge graphs support factual grounding and reasoning in AI systems.
By encoding domain knowledge as a graph, AI systems can leverage structured information
to improve accuracy and reduce hallucinations.

Entities in a knowledge graph are assigned types such as Person, Organization, or Concept.
Relations between entities are typed and directed, forming a labeled directed graph.
A hypergraph generalizes this by allowing a single relation to connect multiple head and tail entities.

Extract candidate entities and relations from the text.
Return only valid JSON matching the required output schema.
Do not invent facts that are not supported by the text.
Include evidence spans for every relation.
